overview: President Trump said he is close to launching a 'massive attack' on Iran, larger than anything in the twelve-night campaign, as UN Secretary-General António Guterres warned the Security Council the Middle East is 'getting out of control' and near 'the edge of the unimaginable.' The threat — widely read as the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex — drove Brent crude above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months and sent shares sliding, alongside Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers and a threat to make the Red Sea a second 'Gate of Tears.' In Washington the House rebuked Trump, passing a war-powers resolution to end the war a day after it voted to fund it — though the Senate split, leaving no binding brake.

keyPoints.text: Trump said he is 'close' to deciding on a 'massive attack' on Iran larger than any yet; UN chief António Guterres warned the Middle East is 'getting out of control.'
The House passed a war-powers resolution ordering Trump to end the war — a second rebuke, a day after voting to fund it — but the Senate split, leaving no binding brake.
Brent crude broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months and shares slid, as two contested chokepoints and 'massive attack' fears priced in.
Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthis attacked Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and threatened to make Bab al-Mandeb a second 'Gate of Tears,' widening the war to a second sea route.
US Central Command said it completed a twelfth consecutive night of strikes on Iran; Al Jazeera earlier reported a strike killed two people, with explosions in Sirik, Ahvaz and Bushehr.
War-risk insurance for Hormuz tankers has hit roughly four times its five-year average, per Al Jazeera, as both chokepoints turn kinetic and pump prices stay above $4 a gallon.
The confirmed US toll stands at 17–18 dead and nearly 100 wounded, as Trump appeared to downplay the deaths online while receiving four of the fallen home.

watch.text: Whether Trump orders the 'massive attack' he says he is close to deciding on — widely read as the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' nuclear complex — the single largest rung of escalation now on the table, and how Iran answers it.

Whether the Senate takes up the House-passed war funding — the ~$95bn budget and $1.15tn military bill — and whether either chamber can align on a binding war-powers measure after the House and Senate split.
Whether the Houthi tanker attacks and the Bab al-Mandeb 'Gate of Tears' threat draw a US or Saudi military response, and how insurers and shippers react to a second live oil chokepoint.

stories.title: Trump says he is 'close' to a 'massive attack' as the UN warns the war is 'out of control'
stories.summary: President Trump said he is close to deciding whether to launch 'a massive attack' on Iran — on a scale larger than the strikes already waged over twelve nights — the Guardian and Axios reported, in remarks that followed the Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers and oil's climb back above $100. The threat, widely read as pointing at the deeply buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang) nuclear complex near Natanz that Trump earlier vowed to hit 'very heavily,' reopens the war's nuclear dimension with IAEA safeguards suspended and no inspectors inside. UN Secretary-General António Guterres — the world body's top official — told the Security Council the Middle East is being pushed to 'the edge of the unimaginable' and that the crisis is 'getting out of control,' pleading for restraint as the fighting risks tipping from a grinding air campaign into an open regional war.
stories.explainer: A 'massive attack' larger than the twelve-night campaign most plausibly means the hardened, roughly 100-metre-deep Pickaxe Mountain site — a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. Striking it implies the largest bunker-busting ordnance and raises the prospect of either failure or an uncontrolled release near nuclear material that locked-out inspectors could not independently assess. Guterres's language matters because a UN chief rarely tells the Security Council to its face that a war is slipping beyond control: it signals that the outside world sees the next rungs — massive strike, Iranian reprisal, wider war — as close to the point where no single leader can halt the climb.

stories.title: House passes a war-powers resolution to end the war — a day after voting to fund it
stories.summary: The US House of Representatives passed a war-powers resolution directing President Trump to end the Iran conflict — the second time it has voted to rein in the war, NPR, the Guardian and Al Jazeera reported, and the first congressional vote to limit the campaign since the interim ceasefire collapsed. The rebuke lands roughly a day after the same chamber adopted a ~$95bn budget to fund the war and a $1.15tn military bill, putting the House on record both bankrolling the campaign and ordering it stopped. Congress as a whole delivered no binding brake: Politico and CNBC reported the House and Senate split on rival war-powers measures, so no joint resolution reached Trump's desk, and any that did would face his veto. Senator Rand Paul, a Kentucky Republican long opposed to the war, captured the disquiet, saying the US is 'worse off than we were before.'
stories.explainer: A war-powers resolution is Congress's constitutional tool to force a president to pull US forces out of hostilities he never obtained a declaration of war for — but it binds only if both chambers pass it and it survives a veto, which needs a two-thirds override. The House passing it while the Senate splits produces a loud political signal with no legal force: it records a majority of the lower chamber against the war and widens the rift with the White House prosecuting it, yet Trump can keep striking. Funding the war one day and voting to end it the next captures a Congress that is furious about the campaign but unwilling — or unable — to actually pull the plug.

stories.title: Oil breaks $100 a barrel as two chokepoints and 'massive attack' fears bite
stories.summary: Brent crude broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months and global shares slid, the Guardian and CNBC reported, as the Houthi attacks on Saudi tankers, Iran's threat to turn the Red Sea's Bab al-Mandeb strait into a second 'Gate of Tears,' and Trump's talk of a 'massive attack' on Iran stacked fresh risk onto an already-stretched market. Analysts tied the move directly to fears that the Houthi blockade could strangle Saudi exports — the supply the market had counted on when Hormuz tightened. War-risk insurance for tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz has climbed to roughly four times its five-year average, Al Jazeera reported, a cost that flows straight into freight rates and, in time, pump prices. US average gasoline remains above $4 a gallon.
stories.explainer: The jump from the $90s to above $100 is what it looks like when a market stops assuming some oil always gets through. With Hormuz already a shooting zone and the Red Sea now contested too, traders can no longer price Saudi Arabia as the reliable relief valve, so the risk premium widens sharply. Insurance is the transmission mechanism: quadrupled war-risk premiums make every voyage costlier whether or not a given tanker is hit, and those costs compound through freight and refining into the price of fuel worldwide — the channel by which a war over two straits reaches households far from the Gulf.

stories.title: Houthis hit Saudi tankers and threaten a second 'Gate of Tears' in the Red Sea
stories.summary: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement attacked a Saudi oil tanker in the Red Sea and claimed to have struck another vessel, the BBC, NBC and Al Jazeera reported — following through on the 'maritime embargo' it declared on Saudi Arabia earlier in the week and turning its threatened second front kinetic. The group warned it would make Bab al-Mandeb — the narrow strait at the Red Sea's southern mouth whose name translates as the 'Gate of Tears' — a live chokepoint for any tanker calling at Saudi ports, layering a second blockade atop Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The strikes rippled straight into markets, helping push oil above $100, and stoked what the BBC called fears of a wider Middle East conflict and deeper global economic damage as the war presses on two oil arteries at once.
stories.explainer: Iran fights asymmetrically, and the Houthis let it open a second maritime front it need not staff itself: where Iran squeezes Hormuz, its Yemeni ally squeezes Bab el-Mandeb and the Red Sea route to Saudi ports. A blockade only reshapes markets once it is enforced — insurers and shipping lines price a declaration as risk but reroute for real once tankers are actually hit. Striking Saudi crude is that threshold, because Saudi Arabia was the one large Gulf producer the market treated as a relief valve when Hormuz tightened; hitting its exports shrinks the cushion that has so far kept the war from becoming a full global oil crisis, which is why two struck ships move the price of oil worldwide.

stories.title: CENTCOM says it completed a twelfth consecutive night of strikes on Iran
stories.summary: US Central Command said it completed its latest strikes against Iran, extending the campaign to a twelfth consecutive night, the US Department of War and Al Jazeera reported, with the military saying it is degrading Tehran's ability to threaten shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. Al Jazeera's earlier live coverage reported that a US strike killed two people, with explosions reported in the Iranian cities of Sirik, Ahvaz and Bushehr. The unbroken run of strikes — now stretching well past a week and a half — has continued even as ceasefire mediators work, Congress votes on the war's cost and its future, and both sides openly threaten each other's civilian infrastructure, underlining that the fighting has settled into a grinding tempo that neither the diplomacy nor the domestic backlash has yet interrupted.
stories.explainer: A twelfth consecutive night is less a single event than evidence of a war that has become routine: the strikes no longer spike or pause around diplomatic openings or congressional votes, they simply continue. Reported civilian deaths and explosions near Bushehr — Iran's largest nuclear power plant — keep two dangers live at once: a mounting human toll that feeds the domestic and legal backlash, and a radiological risk near a fueled reactor that inspectors, locked out since strikes on Natanz, cannot independently assess.

stories.title: House votes to fund the war — a ~$95bn budget and a $1.15tn military bill
stories.summary: A day before it moved to end the war, the US House passed a roughly $95bn Republican budget to fund the Iran campaign and a separate $1.15tn military spending bill that expands cooperation with Israel, the New York Times, Al Jazeera and NPR reported — the House's answer to Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's request for tens of billions more. Both measures passed largely along party lines and face an uncertain Senate. The blueprint unlocks new dollars to replace the roughly $67bn in munitions the Pentagon says it has burned through, against a war Hegseth put at $37.5bn so far — money the House then, in the same week, voted to stop spending via a war-powers resolution.
stories.explainer: Wars run on appropriated dollars, so the fight over funding is the fight over whether the war can continue. The House passing the money looks like Congress endorsing the campaign, but the party-line margin and the 'uncertain Senate' are the real story: appropriations must clear both chambers, and the Senate — where Hegseth met open bipartisan hostility — is where the purse-string lever could still attach conditions or refuse. The $67bn to restock arms is the tell that the US has been firing munitions faster than it can replace them, a material limit on how long the air campaign can last regardless of any vote.

stories.title: US signs a nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia mid-war
stories.summary: The United States signed a civil-nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, the Guardian reported, deepening the strategic relationship with Riyadh in the middle of the Iran war. The timing is striking: Washington is extending nuclear ties to Saudi Arabia even as US bombs fall on Iran's nuclear facilities and threaten the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' complex, and as Iran's Houthi allies attack Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. As the Guardian's Kenneth Roth noted, the agreement offers the Saudis 'the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have' — a contrast sharpened by Riyadh's past signals that it would want enrichment capacity, or its own program, if Iran retained one. Coming now, the deal reads as Washington binding a key Gulf partner closer as the war reorders the region's alignments.
stories.explainer: A civil-nuclear deal is about reactors and fuel, not weapons, but with Saudi Arabia the line is politically fraught: Riyadh has said it would match Iran's nuclear capabilities, so the terms — especially whether the US permits uranium enrichment on Saudi soil — determine whether the deal caps proliferation or nudges it. Signing it while striking Iran's nuclear sites signals that Washington is trying to lock in a compliant Gulf nuclear order on its own terms, and it further entangles Saudi Arabia in a war in which its tankers are already being hit.

stories.title: Iran warns of an 'eye for an eye' if the US destroys its infrastructure
stories.summary: Iran warned it would respond 'eye for an eye' if the US follows through on President Trump's threat to destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, the Guardian reported. The warning answers the mechanical retaliation formula Trump set out a day earlier and hardens it into a mutual one: each side now publicly ties a specific response to the other's action. Iran has already said it would answer such attacks by sparking power outages in the Gulf states that host US troops. The exchange keeps the war's escalation on autopilot — a ladder that climbs a rung with every ship struck and every span or plant hit, with neither leader left much room to step back without appearing to concede.
stories.explainer: 'Eye for an eye' is the same trap as Trump's per-attack formula, seen from the other side: once both leaders have publicly committed to automatic retaliation, following through is default and backing down looks like weakness, so the fighting escalates without either side deciding to escalate. Because Iran's chosen response — blacking out the Gulf states hosting US bases — punishes third parties, it also turns those host governments into pressure points, giving them every incentive to lean on Washington for a ceasefire before the exchange reaches them.

stories.title: Trump receives the fallen — and appears to downplay them online
stories.summary: President Trump attended the dignified transfer of US service members killed in the Middle East as fallen soldiers were returned to American soil, NPR and Military Times reported, even as ABC News reported that Trump appeared to downplay the war's deaths in social-media posts comparing them to casualties in previous conflicts. The Guardian, profiling four of the dead — Angel Rampersad, Michael Swinton, Isabella Gonzales and Tyler Feehan — described them as among '18 US troops killed in the war,' a figure a notch above the 17 confirmed by an earlier ABC/Time tally; the Pentagon has acknowledged nearly 100 wounded, a day after the New York Times reported dozens of injuries had been withheld. The juxtaposition of the solemn ceremony and the dismissive posts captures the political bind the losses have created for a president fighting a war his own voters are turning against.
stories.explainer: The dead and wounded are the war's most politically potent variable at home: each named casualty turns an abstract tally into a concrete cost, feeding the congressional fight over funding and war powers. The toll itself is contested and creeping upward — an earlier count of 17 against the Guardian's reference to 18 reflects the fog around a war fought across several countries — but the direction is clear. A president who both honors the fallen in person and minimizes them online is managing two audiences at once: a public that recoils from the losses and a base he cannot be seen to have led into a costly, unpopular war.

stories.title: 'Pickaxe Mountain' is the likely target of the threatened 'massive attack'
stories.summary: President Trump's talk of a 'massive attack' on Iran has refocused attention on the complex known in the West as 'Pickaxe Mountain' (Kuh-e Kolang), sited near the Natanz enrichment plant and estimated to be buried almost 100 metres inside a mountain, the Guardian reported — far harder to destroy than surface sites already hit, and little understood publicly. Trump had already vowed to attack the underground facility 'very heavily.' The threat lands with IAEA safeguards access suspended since earlier strikes on Natanz and Iran having called off its interim-deal commitments, so no inspectors are inside to gauge the consequences of any strike — a gap sharpened by explosions reported near the Bushehr nuclear plant during the twelfth night.
stories.explainer: Striking a hardened, deeply buried nuclear site is a different order of escalation from bombing shipping or power plants. A facility 100 metres underground is built to survive conventional bombs, so hitting it 'very heavily' implies the largest bunker-busting ordnance and raises the prospect of either failure or an uncontrolled release near sensitive nuclear material. With inspectors locked out, no one — neither side nor the outside world — would have an independent reading of what a strike actually did.

stories.title: Poll shows even Trump voters losing patience with the war
stories.summary: A new poll shows Trump voters losing patience with the Iran war, which Politico described as leaving the president in 'a politically impossible situation,' even as Trump insisted the midterms would not affect his war plan. The finding compounds earlier reporting, by The Economist, that the renewed conflict is the least popular US war since modern polling began — support falling below the low points of the Iraq and Vietnam wars. The erosion within the president's own base underpins the search for a way to terminate the war, the hostile Senate reception Hegseth met, and the House's move to pass a war-powers resolution ordering the fighting stopped even as it voted to fund it.
stories.explainer: Domestic unpopularity is a strategic variable, not just a political one: a president fighting a war the public rejects has less room to escalate and more incentive to seek an exit — but dead soldiers pull the other way, creating pressure to 'finish' the fight rather than be seen abandoning it. When the disaffection reaches the president's own voters, it sharpens that bind with midterms approaching, and it helps explain why a Republican-led House would vote to end a Republican president's war.

stories.title: The war's $37.5bn bill and $88bn ask, now before Congress
stories.summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate the US-Israel war on Iran has cost $37.5bn so far and asked for nearly $88bn more — including roughly $67bn to restock depleted munitions — the Guardian, PBS and Reuters reported, drawing open bipartisan hostility, with Michigan Democrat Gary Peters telling him 'you, sir, are the failure' and Hegseth refusing to give any timeline for the war's end. That request is what the House answered by passing a roughly $95bn war budget and a $1.15tn military bill; the Senate, where the hearing turned hostile, has not, and Congress remains split over whether to fund the war or force its end.
stories.explainer: Once the Pentagon must return to Congress mid-war for tens of billions more, the legislature acquires a financial choke-point it otherwise lacks. The split between a House that funded the war and a Senate that grilled Hegseth over it is now the central domestic fault line — and Hegseth's refusal to name an end date is exactly what hardens senators' resolve to attach conditions or refuse, and what powers the war-powers push to order the campaign stopped outright.

stories.title: Iran's reprisals keep falling on US Gulf allies
stories.summary: Iran's retaliation has continued to fall on the US-aligned states that ring it rather than on American soil directly, with recent reprisals against Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan — Jordan's army said it intercepted four of six Iranian missiles fired Wednesday, with two falling in remote areas, AFP reported. Iran has separately claimed it struck Amazon infrastructure in Bahrain and set a Kuwaiti electricity-and-water plant ablaze, matching its warning that if US strikes persist 'no political border' will be safe. A leaked US assessment concluded Iran retained roughly 70% of its ballistic missiles after the spring campaign.
stories.explainer: Hitting the US through the Gulf states that shelter its bases raises the cost of the American campaign while pressuring those governments to lean on Washington for a ceasefire. A claimed strike on cloud infrastructure widens the target set from bases and power plants to the digital backbone of the region — a way to make hosting American forces and companies politically and economically unbearable.

stories.title: Strikes and Rubio's skepticism keep overshadowing ceasefire talks
stories.summary: US-mediated efforts to salvage the collapsed interim ceasefire, which had moved to Pakistan, remain overshadowed as a twelfth consecutive night of strikes and Secretary of State Marco Rubio's judgment that Iran is 'not serious about a deal' undercut the diplomacy, AP and the Guardian reported. Mediators had pressed a specific proposal — earlier framed as a 10-day ceasefire — to pause the fighting long enough for both sides to step back from the chokepoints, but the effort runs against a hardening war: Hegseth would set no timeline, Iran has called off its interim-deal commitments, and Trump is now weighing a 'massive attack' while Iran warns 'eye for an eye.' Iran says diplomatic contacts continue through intermediaries, leaving negotiation the war's thinnest — and still only — off-ramp.
stories.explainer: With the interim deal dead and IAEA inspectors locked out, there is no standing framework to fall back on, so any de-escalation must be rebuilt from scratch through third parties. Talks that proceed under a twelfth night of bombing, mutual per-attack retaliation formulas and a public US verdict that Tehran is not serious have little leverage: each strike and each hardening statement raises the price of being seen to concede.

stories.title: BBC returns to the Minab school where an opening-day strike killed scores of children
stories.summary: Nearly five months after the war began, the BBC visited the girls' school in Minab, southern Iran, where a strike on the conflict's opening day killed scores of children — the BBC's account put the toll at around 120 — speaking to a teacher and a mother still living with the horror. The report is a rare on-the-ground reckoning with the human cost of the campaign that opened on 28 February with the largest Israeli combat sortie in history, and a reminder that beneath the shifting fronts and funding votes the war's toll on Iranian civilians has been accumulating since its first hours.
stories.explainer: Casualty figures from the war's chaotic opening remain contested — early tallies of the Minab strike ranged higher — and the point of a return visit is less the exact number than the enduring cost: a single strike on a school, months on, still defines the war for the families it touched, the kind of civilian toll that shapes both Iran's resolve and the international judgment of the campaign.

stories.title: Iran's foreign minister recounts the strike that killed Khamenei
stories.summary: Iran's foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, described being pulled from the rubble of a leadership compound after the US-Israeli strike on the first day of the war — the same strike that killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei — and said he has kept constantly on the move since, the Guardian reported. The account is a rare first-person window into Iran's decapitation at the war's outset and how its surviving leadership has operated under threat ever since, landing as Trump weighs a 'massive attack' on Iran's nuclear sites.
stories.explainer: Killing a state's supreme leader on day one is a decapitation strike, and its aftershocks explain much of the war's shape: a leadership that survives by staying mobile is harder to negotiate with, slower to make binding decisions, and more reliant on hardened institutions like the IRGC. It shows why Iran's responses have been fragmented and its diplomatic signals mixed.

stories.title: Israel says 'no interest' in joining, but readies plans
stories.summary: Israel — which with the United States launched the war's opening blow, Operation Epic Fury, in late February — has sent a deliberately mixed message about re-entering the renewed war. Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich said Israel has 'no interest' in joining the US military campaign against Iran, a public signal of restraint, even as CNN reported that Israel is quietly readying plans in case it is drawn back in. Israel has stayed off the battlefield since the fighting reignited on 8 July, leaving the current phase a US-Iran duel, but has warned it will respond 'with full force' if Iranian weapons strike its territory — a stance the newly signed US-Israel military-cooperation expansion in the House bill only deepens.
stories.explainer: Israel rejoining is the fastest way to widen the war, because it doubles the fronts Iran must fight and risks reactivating Hezbollah and other 'Axis of Resistance' militias. Smotrich's 'no interest' and CNN's 'readying plans' are not contradictory: they describe a government that wants to stay out but is hedging against a single errant Iranian missile that could trip its 'full force' tripwire.

notes: Evening refresh of 2026-07-23 (previous cut generated 2026-07-23T11:07Z). The manual RSS refresh via scripts/fetch-news.mjs was again blocked by the environment's egress policy (403 on all news hosts), so this draws on the workflow-committed feed (data/news.json, generated 2026-07-23T18:37Z; freshest item 18:25Z). New since the previous briefing: President Trump said he is 'close' to deciding on a 'massive attack' on Iran larger than any yet, widely read as the buried 'Pickaxe Mountain' complex, and UN Secretary-General António Guterres told the Security Council the Middle East is at 'the edge of the unimaginable' and 'getting out of control'; the US House passed a war-powers resolution directing Trump to end the war (its second such vote) a day after voting to fund it, while the House and Senate split on rival measures leaving no binding brake; Brent crude broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months and shares slid; the Houthis attacked Saudi tankers and threatened to make Bab al-Mandeb a second 'Gate of Tears,' with Hormuz war-risk insurance running ~4x its five-year average; and CENTCOM said it completed a twelfth consecutive night of strikes. Featured stories are ranked by systemic significance, scale, breadth, novelty and continuity; overview, keyPoints and stories describe the same events at different altitudes. Contested claims — the threatened 'massive attack' and its likely target, the war's $37.5bn cost and ~$88bn/$95bn/$1.15tn funding figures, casualty and injury figures (17–18 dead, ~100 wounded, two reportedly killed on the twelfth night, the ~120 Minab-school toll), the '70%' missile-retention estimate, oil and pump prices, insurance multiples, the depth and nature of 'Pickaxe Mountain,' Jordan's interception count, the terms of the US-Saudi nuclear deal, and Iranian- or Houthi-announced actions — are attributed to their sources.
